query: What is overfitting and how do I fix it?
answer:
Based on the retrieved documents (Overfitting and Regularisation, Confidence Intervals, ANOVA and Post-hoc Tests), here is the answer to 'What is overfitting and how do I fix it?':

Overfitting occurs when a model learns noise in training data and fails to generalise. Signs: train accuracy much higher than test accuracy, gap greater than 15 percent is concerning. query: How do I test if my time series is stationary?
answer:
Based on the retrieved documents (Time Series Stationarity and ARIMA, Time Series Decomposition and Forecasting, Confidence Intervals), here is the answer to 'How do I test if my time series is stationary?':

A stationary time series has constant mean, variance, and autocorrelation structure over time. ARIMA models require stationarity. ADF (Augmented Dickey-Fuller) test: if p < 0.05, series is stationary. To make stationary: first differencing subtracts lag-1 value, log transform stabilises variance, se...

query: What is the difference between Type I and Type II error?
answer:
Based on the retrieved documents (Hypothesis Testing, Exploratory Data Analysis, ANOVA and Post-hoc Tests), here is the answer to 'What is the difference between Type I and Type II error?':

A hypothesis test evaluates two mutually exclusive statements about a population. H0 (null hypothesis) states no effect exists. H1 (alternative) states an effect exists. The p-value is the probability of observing results as extreme as the data assuming H0 is true. If p-value < alpha (usually 0.05),...

query: Which feature importance method should I use for Random Forest?
answer:
Based on the retrieved documents (Feature Importance and Selection, Random Forest and Ensemble Methods, Overfitting and Regularisation), here is the answer to 'Which feature importance method should I use for Random Forest?':

Random Forest feature importance measures mean decrease in impurity across all trees. Limitation: biased toward high-cardinality and continuous features. Permutation importance: more reliable, measures score drop when feature is shuffled randomly. SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations): game-theoretic...
